definitions.definition: The process of designing and maintaining an environment in which individuals, working together in groups, efficiently accomplish selected aims. It involves Planning, Organising, Staffing, Directing, and Controlling.
Completing the task on time — doing the right things. Concerned with the end result.
Completing the task at minimum cost — doing things right. Concerned with optimum use of resources.
The process of integrating efforts of different individuals to secure unity of action in the pursuit of common goals. It is the essence of management.
Management cannot be seen physically but its presence is felt when targets are met, employees are satisfied, and there is orderliness instead of chaos.
Management activities are common to all organisations — economic, social, or political. A petrol pump needs to be managed as much as a hospital or a cricket team.
Management involves three dimensions simultaneously — management of Work, People, and Operations.
Survival (earn enough revenue to cover costs), Profit (earn reasonable profit after covering costs and risks), Growth (increase sales, employees, products, and market share).
Obligations towards society — providing quality products, pollution control, employment generation, opening schools and healthcare centres.
Helping employees achieve their individual goals — good salaries, perks, working conditions, training, and career development opportunities.
Chairman, CEO, COO — they lay down policies and objectives, formulate strategies, and are responsible for the welfare and survival of the organisation.
Departmental heads, Plant Superintendents — they carry out plans as per top-level policies, organise divisional activities, and link top and lower levels.
Foremen, Supervisors — they allocate tasks to workers, ensure quality and quantity of output, and communicate worker grievances upward.

points.b: Completing the job at minimum cost

points.a: May or may not be efficient
points.b: May or may not be effective

scenarios.scenario: A production manager produces 10,000 shirts by the deadline but spends ₹160/shirt instead of the budgeted ₹150/shirt.
scenarios.principle: Completed on time (effective) but exceeded cost (not efficient)

scenarios.scenario: A factory produces goods at very low cost, but the order is delivered a week late.
scenarios.principle: Minimum cost (efficient) but missed deadline (not effective)

scenarios.scenario: Targets are met, employees are happy, and there is orderliness instead of chaos — but no one can point to management physically.
scenarios.principle: Cannot be seen but its presence is felt through results

scenarios.scenario: A hospital, a cricket team, and a factory all need someone to plan, organise, and coordinate activities.
scenarios.principle: Needed in every type of organisation — business, social, or sports

scenarios.scenario: The CEO of a company formulates long-term strategies and interacts with government officials about new regulations.
scenarios.principle: Lay down policies, formulate strategies, interact with external environment

scenarios.scenario: A foreman at a steel plant assigns tasks to workers, checks quality of output, and reports worker issues to the manager above.
scenarios.principle: Allocate tasks, ensure quality, communicate grievances upward

scenarios.scenario: The marketing, production, and finance departments work in silos with conflicting goals, leading to delays and confusion.
scenarios.principle: Coordination integrates group efforts and ensures unity of action

scenarios.scenario: A company generates employment, uses eco-friendly packaging, and donates to education — beyond profit-making.
scenarios.principle: Obligations towards society beyond just earning profit

scenarios.scenario: An MBA graduate runs a company successfully without any mandatory license or registration from a professional body.
scenarios.answer: Management is NOT a full-fledged Profession
scenarios.principle: No mandatory degree, no compulsory registration, no statutory code of conduct

scenarios.scenario: McDonald's changed its menu for the Indian market, removing beef and introducing McAloo Tikki.
scenarios.principle: Must adapt to changing social, economic, and cultural environment
